messages.subject: [R-SIG-Mac]building packages for mac
messages.body_plain:
Hi,

I have an R package written for a joint project. My collaborators in
forestry and botany use macs. I don't have mac access in my office, and my
collaborators are not terribly familiar with R. For short run, I am
wondering if someone here could compile the package for mac for us, we
would appriciate very much. For long run, I wold like to build this on my
own when I get access to mac in the lab. Is there documentations on how to
do this (just like Ripley's Rtools for windows)?

Thanks,

Jun

messages.subject: [R-SIG-Mac]Launching R 1.2.3
messages.body_plain:
Dear Peter,

I'm investigating on that. My actual suspect is that CarbonLib 1.2.5 has 
some incompatibility with system 8.6 even if it should not as the Apple 
doc says.

I'm trying to install an 8.6 machine to try to debug this problem. Have 
you the possibility to check how much stack R allocates. There is a 
general phrase in the CarbonLib docs that says: "on older systems your 
application  may require more stack". If you agree I can recompile R 
adding more stack to see if the problem is really the stack or something 
else. Let me know.

(Of course, in any sense, the best strategy is to update the System to 
9.1 even if it is quite "heavy" on old machines)

Stefano

messages.subject: [R-SIG-Mac]Re: CarbonLib 1.2.5
messages.body_plain:
Peter wrote

>>  I have a 6100 with Sonnet G3 card, 72 Mb RAM, RamDoubler, OS 8.6, ...

At Sat, 5 May 2001 12:24:10 +0200, Stefano wrote:
>I'm investigating on that. My actual suspect is that CarbonLib 1.2.5 has
>some incompatibility with system 8.6 even if it should not as the Apple
>doc says.
...
>
>(Of course, in any sense, the best strategy is to update the System to
>9.1 even if it is quite "heavy" on old machines)

CarbonLib 1.2.5 also seems to solve various incompatibility problems 
if it's installed under Mac OS 9.0.4. This configuration is also, 
however, quite "heavy" compared to any 8.x option. In particular, 72 
Mb isn't going to be enough -- OS 9.x just barely runs under 64 Mb, 
and really 96 or 128 is necessary to do anything. But as I recall the 
memory footprint of 9.04 is a few Mb better than 9.1.

As for performance, just remember the two standard rules for a more 
responsive Mac OS system: turn off file sharing and virtual memory.
-- 
-----------------------

messages.subject: [R-SIG-Mac]R 1.2.3 Unix and XFree86
messages.body_plain:
Just wanted to note that R builds successfully
using XFree86 from http://mrcla.com/XonX/ .
(Thanks to tips from Jan on how to build loadable
shared libraries using Apple's version of gcc.)
I just got Xtools from Tenon, and the version built
using XFree86 ran perfectly. It would be worth
checking that a binary compiled using Xtools ran
for somewone using XFree86 as well. (I don't see why
it shouldn't.)

Has anyone heard about a pdf graphics driver under
development for R?

-Steve

PS I have the ratfor preprocessor compiled for OS X.

messages.subject: [R-SIG-Mac]patch for System 8.6
messages.body_plain:
I have put a patch that will be available tomorrow on CRAN or can be 
found directly at

http://www.eco-dip.unimi.it/R/cran/base/8.6_patch


This patch is needed only by System 8.6 users that experiments a System 
Error 3.

This patch only fixes a problem with a function call that is not 
available under MacOS System 8.6.  No other bugs have been fixed.

In any case you should download the original rm123.sit archive at CRAN 
and then substitute the file "R" (that is the R application) with the 
one you find in the patched archive.

If you experiment any other problem, please let me know and they will be 
fixed in release 1.3.0


Stefano

messages.subject: [R-SIG-Mac]problems with printing
messages.body_plain:
With the latest version R1.2.3 for Mac, I encountered some problems with producing hardcopies of my graphics.

(1) Each time I choose the menu 'Print...' of the graphics window,  a graphics is printed out, independent of if I press the button 'print' or 'cancel'.

(2) Using the menu again for printing out, a light gray background (hardly visible) is printed on the hardcopy.

(3) Lines and symbols appear less thin, than if I use postscript().

(4) Points (2) and (3) happen also, if I copy and paste the graphics into a word document.

All these problems don't occur, if I create first a postscript with postscript() and print it afterwards.

I work on a G4 with MacOS 9.0.4 (CarbonLib 1.3.1), and I reserved enough memory for R.

Any suggestions?
Christof

--

Christof Bigler
Mountain Forest Ecology (ETH)
e-mail: bigler@fowi.ethz.ch

In the usual place

http://www.stat.ucla.edu/~deleeuw/R

there is now a version of R-1.2.3 which has been compiled with gcc/ g77,
and which uses tcl/tk 8.3.3. The earlier versions used f2c and tcl/tk 
8.0.

The file merely contains the standard /usr/local/lib/R, without any 
additional packages.
You can get the additional packages from the earlier distributions.

It is quite possible, quite likely even, that running the program will 
not
work because it requires dylibs that you do not have. A sprinkling of
dylibs is in the same directory. If you need something else, drop me a
line.

The g77 I use comes from http://www-jlc.kek.jp/~fujiik/macosx/10.0.X/
which has oodles of great tools. tcl/tk 8.3.3 now compiles out of the box
on OS X.

messages.subject: [R-SIG-Mac]compiling fortran code of contributed packages using absoft f77 and lnk
messages.subject_clean: compiling fortran code of contributed packages using absoft f77 and lnk
messages.body_plain:
DeaR mac users

I tried to compile the fortran code of the "splancs" package using 
Absoft's Fortran compiler f77 and combine the object files into a 
shared library using Absoft's lnk. I could create the library without 
any problem but
when I tried to access the library from R strange things happen.

I can load the modules contained in the library by the R's dyn.load().

But when I check whether a particular Fortran routine, say "areapl", 
is accessible to R by using
>  is.loaded(symbol.For("areapl"))
[1] FALSE
>

Strange enough if I test
>  is.loaded(symbol.C("areapl"))
[1] TRUE

I get the answer that areapl is accessible as a C module.
>From this I conclude that lnk gives the shared library some attribute 
which makes R think that the library contains C code.

For contributed packages containing C code I did not experience any 
problems, ie. acc and lnk created a shared library accessible to R 
(e.g. I could install geoR, available from 
http://www.maths.lancs.ac.uk/~ribeiro/geoR.html, on my G4).

Now my question is: how can I control that lnk creates a shared 
library consisting of FORTRAN routines. Does anybody have a hint?

Andreas

For MPW/Absoft users I include the contents of the make file

Makefile     = # "{TargetName}".make
ObjDir_ppc   = :

  "{AbsoftLibraries}libfmath"

  "{ObjDir_ppc}krnqne.o" Â
  "{ObjDir_ppc}krnqrt.o" Â
  "{ObjDir_ppc}nndist.o" Â
  "{ObjDir_ppc}plarea.o" Â
  "{ObjDir_ppc}sekhat.o" Â
  "{ObjDir_ppc}sort.o" Â
  "{ObjDir_ppc}sort2.o" Â
  "{ObjDir_ppc}stkhat.o" Â
  "{ObjDir_ppc}stsecal.o" Â
  "{ObjDir_ppc}tribble.o" Â
  "{ObjDir_ppc}twodimmse.o" Â

# Linkage rule

"{TargetName}" Å¸Å¸ {PPC_OBJECTS} {makefile}
  lnk -O -xm library -o "{TargetName}" -export "{TargetName}.x" Â

  lnk -O -xm sharedLibrary -o "{TargetName}" -export "{TargetName}.x" Â

> Now my question is: how can I control that lnk creates a shared library

>

I suspect that the absoft compiler do not user "underlines" in the 
function names. R for MacOS actually add a "_" symbol to fortran 
functions. This is because Mac f2c uses this convention.

Can you please try to do this witht he absoft compiler ? I do not own 
this compiler but it would be very important to understand what is going 
on.

</excerpt>umh, I never noticed it and I try to fix it too


<color><param>0000,0000,DEB7</param>

</color><excerpt>

(3) Lines and symbols appear less thin, than if I use postscript().

</excerpt>this is due to the different devices' resolutions. Macintosh
device is only 72 dpi while ps, if I remember well, is 300 dpi or more.

<color><param>0000,0000,DEB7</param>

</color><excerpt>

(4) Points (2) and (3) happen also, if I copy and paste the graphics
into a word document.


</excerpt>yes, this is because also printing has the same resolution
of macintosh device. In any case the postscript and the forthcoming
pdf device are "first best" for graphics printing.

<color><param>0000,0000,DEB7</param>

</color>Stefano=

messages.body_plain:
I ran into this same problem using the Absoft windows compiler. Absoft
Tech Support and Brian Ripley were both very helpful. After you make sure
that you are consistent with upper and lower case, and with the trailing
underscore, use .C and not .Fortran. Here was Brian's solution:

~~~~~~~

Date: Tue, 2 May 2000 07:05:15 +0100 (BST)
From: Prof Brian D Ripley <ripley@stats.ox.ac.uk>
To: Peter Macdonald <pdmmac@mcmail.cis.mcmaster.ca>
Subject: Re: dll's for R (fwd)

On Mon, 1 May 2000, Peter Macdonald wrote:

> It looks as though is.loaded() recognizes the symbol but .Fortran()
> doesn't. The symbol name agrees with the name found by pedump.

Use .C for `Foreign' compilers.  That does no mapping.  I thought I
suggested that yesterday (and I did put it in readme.packages yesterday).

*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*
 Peter D.M. Macdonald, D.Phil.      McMaster University
 Professor of Math & Statistics     Hamilton, Ontario, Canada L8S 4K1
*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*~*


On Mon, 28 May 2001, Stefano Iacus wrote:

> 
> 
> 
> > Now my question is: how can I control that lnk creates a shared library 
> > consisting of FORTRAN routines. Does anybody have a hint?
> >
> 
> I suspect that the absoft compiler do not user "underlines" in the 
> function names. R for MacOS actually add a "_" symbol to fortran 
> functions. This is because Mac f2c uses this convention.
> 
> Can you please try to do this witht he absoft compiler ? I do not own 
> this compiler but it would be very important to understand what is going 
> on.
>

messages.body_plain:
Thanks to the hints I got, I could easily solve the problem with 
Absoft's Fortran compiler f77.

The option -N15 tells the compiler to add trailing underscores to any 
function and subroutine names. The Fortran routines can then be 
called by R's .Fortran().


>I suspect that the absoft compiler do not user "underlines" in the 
>function names. R for MacOS actually add a "_" symbol to fortran 
>functions. This is because Mac f2c uses this convention.
>
>Can you please try to do this witht he absoft compiler ? I do not 
>own this compiler but it would be very important to understand what 
>is going on.

-- 
Andreas Papritz, Friedenstrasse 15, CH-8400 Winterthur
Tel 052 212 0763, 052 212 0953; Fax 052 212 0953
